edges.explanation: OBR 4% and NBER 6-8% GDP reduction is the strongest available evidence that Brexit damage is real and measured
The scale of GDP loss directly supports the claim that single market membership is the economically optimal recovery path
4-8% GDP loss is the upstream cause of the fiscal cascade through every other domain
4-8% GDP damage directly contradicts the Conservative position of maintaining the Brexit settlement with no closer alignment
Measured GDP damage contradicts Reform's claim that Brexit offers opportunities to be grasped
All FTAs combined at less than 1% GDP vs 4-8% lost definitively proves FTAs cannot compensate
FTA arithmetic being definitive supports single market membership as the only policy that recovers the loss
FTAs being worth less than 1% means surrendering independent FTA policy for a customs union costs very little
0.3% recovery from the reset against 4-8% loss proves the reset is necessary but grossly insufficient
Recovering one-tenth of the damage while maintaining red lines proves the red lines are the constraint not the EU's willingness
EU being 41% of exports and the gravity model confirming proximity dominance proves distant FTAs cannot substitute for EU access
£358bn EU trade being the dominant relationship justifies customs union as the highest-return trade policy
CER's assessment that Labour tolerates damage to avoid political risk directly supports the claim that red lines are political not economic
£5-10bn cost for £100-200bn recovery is the most compelling cost-benefit ratio in the entire manifesto
The 10-20x return on budget contributions directly justifies single market membership as the long-term goal
Small firms reporting EU trade getting harder confirms the ongoing damage is real and worsening not recovering
Business evidence of worsening trade friction justifies customs union to eliminate border checks
Confirmed damage at 4-8% supports the claim that single market membership is the recovery path
Confirmed damage cascading through other domains supports the cross-domain fiscal constraint claim
FTAs not compensating means single market access is the only route to meaningful GDP recovery
FTA failure directly supports customs union as the pragmatic first step
Reset insufficiency proves the red lines accept permanent damage
Customs union being net positive directly supports the customs union policy
Freedom of movement being the most costly red line directly supports the claim that red lines are political choices
Freedom of movement unlocking economic recovery supports single market as the long-term goal requiring FoM acceptance
Single market policy depends on the claim that it is the economically optimal position
Customs union as stepping stone depends on the claim that closer integration recovers GDP
Annual Brexit impact reporting depends on the claim that political choices not economic realities are preventing recovery
Customs union policy depends on the finding that it is net positive despite losing FTA independence
Youth mobility as a step depends on freedom of movement being the binding constraint that must be gradually loosened
No closer alignment directly contradicts the customs union policy supported by the trade evidence
Maintaining Brexit settlement contradicts the long-term single market goal
No customs union red line contradicts the evidence that customs union is the highest-return trade policy
No single market and no FoM red lines contradict the long-term economically optimal position
Bespoke customs union proposal directly matches the evidence-based stepping stone policy
Long-term single market objective matches the evidence-based long-term goal
Youth mobility scheme directly matches the evidence-based policy
Rejoining the EU goes further than EEA but in the same direction as the evidence supports
Scrapping EU legislation and abandoning treaties contradicts every piece of trade evidence
Further divergence from the EU is the opposite direction to what the evidence supports
Customs union is the stepping stone that makes eventual single market membership politically achievable
Youth mobility normalises people movement, gradually building consent for freedom of movement
CER assessment of Labour tolerating damage confirms the reset is insufficient because the political constraints are binding
EU being 41% of exports and £358bn in trade means a customs union eliminating border checks on this volume is clearly net positive
Reset recovering only 0.3% without freedom of movement confirms FoM is the binding constraint that limits economic recovery
Brexit damage cascading across all domains justifies the policy of making the cost visible through annual reporting

cross_domain_edges.explanation: Brexit reducing GDP by 4-8% shrinks the tax base, directly constraining the fiscal space available for every other domain's spending needs
Freedom of movement enabling NHS and care staffing reinforces the immigration finding that restricting movement harms public services
Single market membership restoring workforce mobility would help resolve the NHS overseas recruitment dependency
4-8% GDP loss shrinks the economy from which defence and all other spending must be funded, directly contributing to the £80-130bn gap
Customs union and closer EU alignment enables EU electricity market membership which improves grid interconnection and energy security
Youth mobility and Erasmus rejoining directly support the education domain's international student and researcher mobility policies
